sentences.text: Well, I remember that day very clearly.
We were, as I said, we were the last transport from the-- we were, personally, put into the last transport of the ghetto.
And we had to walk for a while in the city.
Naturally, we didn't know why.
They said that they did take us to-- to a labor camps, and families together.
Situation will be much better.
We believed them.
And then we ended up at the cattle wagons.
We did not, in our nightmares, would visualize that we are going to be hauled into those cattle wagons.
And we are going to be locked from outside.
There were hardly any windows.
And then, that we are going to go for three days and three nights without food, without-- without-- without drinks.
They did not give us anything during this ride.
They did not allow us to have bathroom stops.
We were locked all the way.
And we had to make everything in those wagons.
And we traveled on the horrible conditions for three-- I call it on the way to the grave.
In my-- I have a narrative in my book, and I called this ride, on the way to the grave.
It was the grave, after that, in Auschwitz, for many of us who arrived.
Although the people or those who fell into the category of not being useful.
So you know, my memories while riding this train are very vivid.
I remember we were like sardines, you know.
It was my father was next to me, my mother.
My father was a very strong personality.
Where she was-- he was a very brave and courageous person.
And at night, I couldn't sleep, you know, my thoughts went wild.
I always-- I put the questions, just like an obsession.
Why didn't we escape?
Why didn't we escape?
And that went on and on and on.
And then, I could feel, at night, the tears of my father running on me.
And he said something.
He said, we were fooled.
And now it's too late.
The first time I heard him say that.
And when you are treated like that, you doubt that it's not something better maybe is coming.
But it might be something worse.
And surely enough, after three days and nights of this ride, I can tell you how some people came out of this, you know, older people, and infants.
I mean, they were sick and, pardon me, for the word, but our wagons was filled with excrement and everything else in there.
And then finally, the train stopped.
And I speak German.
And I heard, you know, the voices, you know, from outside.
These were the guards-- German.
And I was really scared, and so were we.
And then suddenly, this heavy doors, you know, of these cattle wagons were opened, and the latch, the bolt, was released.
And then it started-- my worst, my nightmare.
Because part of the nightmares, you know, are of different intensity.
But that was the first nightmare in Auschwitz I experienced.
You see these guards coming in and hauling you out with short little rubber sticks-- out, out.
You know, like-- I think that if you have cattle and you like animals, you don't treat people like that.
They were hauling us up.
You know, first of all some people were weakened, you know, some older people-- out, out, out.
Beating them if they-- they were beating us if we didn't move fast enough, you know.
So we had to go fast, fast, fast.
Out, out, out.
That's the world we heard from the cattle wagons.
And then we had to-- we-- we were standing on a platform.
And I never forget that, because as I was standing on the platform, I looked and I could see, you know, from a distance these-- these huge flames belching from chimneys.
And I felt a terrible sweet-- it's-- a-- a-- sweet odor of burning flesh.
And I was wondering what are they burning there?
I had no idea that those were the crematory ovens, you know?
I had no idea of that.
But it was a strange, sickening sweetish odor of burning flesh-- because I arrived in Auschwitz-Birkenau.
And you see the Birkenau was the place where they had the-- the gas chambers.
And then was-- we were standing on this platform.
We were started, the-- the second nightmare-- the selection, separation.
Men were separated from women.
That's terrible, you know-- suddenly, you know, your father is separated from you.
And then, we-- then we had to-- then I have seen, you know, children-- they were asking the children's ages, you know.
And the mothers didn't know how separating children from their mothers, you know.
And they did not-- they told the truth.
If you told the truth, and you said that your child was 13 years old, she went to the gas chambers.
If she was-- if she was 10 years old, five years old, not to speak of infants and pregnant woman, they went straight to the gas chambers.
But at that point, I didn't know.
I wasn't informed what's going to happen.
So all what I could see at that stage is like a quick separation, men from women, children taken from their mothers.
And then I see a-- a Jewish medical committee, with-- headed by Dr.
Josef Mengele.
I have seen, personally, Dr.
Joseph Mengele.
And if any Nazi criminals should have been really brought to justice, it was not-- he.
Because he sent many of my family members to the gas chambers.
And-- and-- I never forget Dr.
Mengele, you know.
I never will forget his looks.
Interestingly enough, he was for the Aryan race-- he had a dark hair and dark eyebrows.
But there was-- he has impeccable uniforms and his boots were shining.
But what it was very frightening is that cold, callous look on his eyes.
I felt like a cold, callous look-- a cruel, cold, callous look.
And then he was holding the stick, you know.
And he was pointing to the left-- asking also the children's ages.
And I could see him pointing to the left, pointing to the right.
Now, I could see many-- all our family members, you know, with whom we were together, all the old people-- my mother's aunts, uncles, nobody turned back.
All the pregnant women in our family were-- were pointed to the left.
All the little children in our family-- there's nobody, no pregnant woman, no small child in our family or infant ever came back.
Because they were all exterminated.
And I could just see him with the stick pointing, pointing.
He looked at me straight in the eye, and pointed me to the right.
I was 18 years old, I was very strong.
I looked like I am good for slave labor-- I  am useful, see?
The other side considered the little kids, what can they do?
Can they drag the corpses and dig the pits?
No.
They can go to the gas chambers.
What is that grandmother is going to go?
Is she going to do the job that they want to?
No.
Maybe some people cannot take.
Here is a young person who is strong-- she can do it, you know?
That's-- I mean, that's-- I tell you now, the story what it was.
And it was a nightmarish thing, a very painful thing, when family started to run after each other.
And there were shots in the air, there were beatings.
Some of them were shot because they tried to run.
I never have seen somebody really shot in cold blood.
You know, I was standing there, and I was so shocked, because I thought to myself, this is a nightmare.
Magda, wake up, this cannot happen.
And you know, everything happened so fast, fast.
And at one point, you know, I could see-- everyone was separated from me, you know?
My family was separated from me.
And I was by myself.
At one point, I looked and I was there among-- you know, in a column of women.
And I looked back, and I have seen my father with my uncle marching.
I didn't know where they march or where they are going.
And suddenly, I was with-- with women whom I didn't know.
There were several whom I knew from Cluj.
There was also a schoolmate of mine there, you know?
But all my family was gone.
You know how you feel?
It was a terrible feeling.
And then-- then, the nightmare came more and more.
So finally, those people-- then they were-- you know what they did?
They came ambulances with the Red Cross in it.
And they-- to take away the sick and the invalids, you know?
But this was just a camouflage, so that they go willingly to be gassed.
They came with a Red Cross ambulances, that they are going to take the sick.
And then they said that--
But you know, there was one thing.
When we-- when we came down from the wagons, we had to leave everything inside.
And there were Polish prisoners who were supposed to come and take everything out and put in the Kanada warehouses in Auschwitz, you know?
And those Polish prisoners, you know, told the mothers, give away your children-- they are going to be better treated.
Because they knew, if the mother doesn't give away, she's going to go into the gas.
They try to persuade the mother, you know, who has an infant-- give your infant away.
She's going to go with her infant to the gas chambers.
And we were accompanied also by the special Hungarian police.
Now, what I have seen, my first impression of Auschwitz, I make you an eyewitness.
You know, my first impression of Auschwitz-- I see here a big camp.
I see these-- these concrete columns connected with barbed wire fence.
On the top of each column there is a light.
And inside where I was standing, you know, by the platform, I see women inside who were-- who were-- their hair was-- was stripped, and they were in rags.
And they came, you know, they have seen us, to beg for food.
And I thought, at my first thought, that this must be the-- maybe a mental asylum or something.
You know, the way they looked?
I didn't realize that I am going to be pretty soon in that-- in that situation.
And so what happened then, I found out later, you know, I dragged corpses in Auschwitz.
What did I do for seven weeks?
Dig death pits in clay ground.
You know, to dig the pits and throw in the warm corpses?
The warm corpses.
If the-- if the-- any warm corpse was-- was stayed warm maybe in the gas chamber, it was thrown in.
But there were killings behind the gas chambers.
People were shot.
I mean you-- you entered into a nightmare where you saw deaths, deaths, and deaths was with me.
In all the camps, death was right accompanying me at all times, every single day.
So what happened to me?
Maybe you will ask what happened to the others?
I can tell you their story, because I made promises for them that if I could come back alive, I'm never going to let them be forgotten.
And I'm going to tell you their story, because I was with them and I have seen them dying and asking me, if you survive Auschwitz, please don't let us be forgotten.
And I'll never let them-- I am not going to ever let them be forgotten.
And I have great respect and admiration for you that you do this program.
I think we need something like that.
And I think that you are wise to do that while the survivors are alive, because you know, everyone hopes to live 100 years old, but some survivors were much older than I was when I got there.
So you see, we are getting older.
And then one day somebody is going to say, well, this is just a fairy tale.
As long as I'm alive, I'm never going to let that happen.
And you people are bringing a great-- a-- a-- a great contribution to mankind by doing what you are doing.
I feel privileged that you came to interview me.
Now let's leave those others, and we will talk about the gas chambers.
And we are going to talk about this-- I am going to tell you some stories.
But what happened to me.
Now it's me.
I was pointed to the right.
First of all, we had to march for a while.
And then we came to the-- to a room, which was the dressing room, you know.
And we had to enter this room and give all, leave all-- first of all, we couldn't take anything from the wagons.
Now we were robbed of all our clothing.
We had to leave all our clothing in that room.
And then we had to enter the bathroom, bath house.
I was fortunate, because on the other side of the bath house, it was written bath house, but that was the gas chamber.
But in my side, it was the bathroom.
Now, my nightmare, the horrible nightmare-- and I can never forget that, you know-- I was standing stark naked.
We had to take all our clothing off, and go into-- into the-- before we go to the bathroom, to the shaving room, where they shaved your hair from your head.
And I entered the shaving room, and I was hiding myself.
You know, I wasn't even naked in front of anybody.
And I go into this room and there's the SS woman, who is with an SS guard who was courting her in that room.
And she sees me coming in, she had a really fun.
I had long hair and the two braids.
And she had a smile on her, because she knew she was going to take the shears right into my hair.
She didn't take the trouble to take my braids down.
But she put the shields right into my hair.
And I was pleading, please leave me.
And I will never forget-- please leave me a little hair.
Please leave me a little hair.
And she shaved all, and then she pulled it, you know, so parts of it that strips were left.
And the others-- I mean, it-- it was a terrible experience.
And then I was so shocked, you know, I just felt a little kind of a little dull sound, as my whole crown of hair, you know, fell on the ground.
I looked back and I said, Magda, this is a nightmare.
It couldn't happen.
I was in a-- I couldn't even cry.
I was shocked.
And then as I was standing, I feel a sharp pain in my back, you know?
One of the guards hit me because I didn't go now fast to the shower room.
And then you were pushed in the shower room.
Do you think you can take a shower, all the while all you hear is out, out.
[NON-ENGLISH].
You can hardly get a little water, and you are chased away from that shower room.
And then you go into the dressing room.
Now the dressing-- see, all our good clothing were taken away, and what we got were the rags, you know, the-- the worst clothing.
And if you want to know what I get, are you interested, how I came out from this room?
I got a dark blue dress, long, I was very lucky.
God was with me that it was up to my ankles, you know?
Because it was cold there at the roll call every morning.
And I-- we didn't get any underwear.
That dress, that was all you got.
I got a shoe, which had a shoelace, a high shoe.
And it had orthopedic inlays.
I have very healthy foot.
It hurt me every time I stepped on that shoe.
That's what I got.
And then my hair cropped up.
So visualize, visualize how I looked.
I still, at this point, I couldn't even cry.
I thought, I am-- I am sleeping.
That couldn't be.
You know, and I just have to wake up.
The third thing that can happen that you were hung in public, so you see what happens to somebody, to you, if you try that.
So escaping Auschwitz was suicidal.
I don't know if somebody tried to escape Auschwitz, I don't know how many survived which were those escapees of Auschwitz.
Now, we came out from the paint room, and, you know, we looked at each other.
And it's an interesting thing, you know?
Some of the people started laughing.
It's like an abnormal reaction.
And because you see-- you see the other person.
You don't see yourself, because you don't have a mirror.
But somebody sees you and starts laughing, how ridiculous you look, you know?
And I could never laugh.
I didn't think it was-- we were like tragic clowns.
That's the word for it.
We came out from that like tragic clowns.
And then from there on, we were led to the barracks, you know.
And when we reached the barracks, I could see-- that was June 1st, in June, 1944.
And I could see there was blades, little blades of grass, you know, growing out in front of the barracks.
And I could see people pulling out the grass and eating it.
There was-- it was that hunger there, you know?
And now under this conditions, the way you were, you get into the barracks-- you want to hear how my barracks look?
You want to have the story?
Well, in-- when I got to Auschwitz, the-- the great-- I could have gotten a worse time.
In 1944, the greatest Jewish extermination was going on.
And people came, you know-- Jewish people were transported from all over Europe, and from the different countries occupied by Hitler.
So they did not have any more, nothing in the barrack, nothing when I came in.
They had no more wooden planks, you know, no more beds, nothing, just the barren floor.
If the barrack was big, it could accommodate 500 people.
And there were two entrances, and there was a door in the middle, a separating door.
Now, you know, this barrack couldn't normally accommodate 500 people, but they made it accommodate, because we were like sardines.
You know, at night you had to push up your feet and-- and you'd be in a fetal position, because everyone was, you know, next to each other.
And not to speak of, they was very poorly made.
And when it was raining the rain came into the barracks.
We were given pails, you know, to push out the rain from the barracks, to actually take out the rain with pails from the barracks.
Now, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was-- there was an incident that I will talk to you about, which I will never forget in the barrack I was.
So we were in the barrack through the night and I had dreams that I was home, and I was combing my hair.
And then suddenly, pitch dark in there, and it's cold in those parts of Germany.
You just have that little dress there, you know, at night.
And there were about three, four people for a blanket-- you know-- they were fighting for the blanket.
You know what happened after work?
People were fighting, they were rushing into these barracks to get the blanket.
You know?
I mean it was fight and fight and fight, fierce fight for survival.
So then 4 o'clock in the morning, it was a nightmare.
The guards came in, you hear, [NON-ENGLISH], with those sticks, and take us out. 4 o'clock in the morning.
It was cold.
And we had to have roll call, for hours before you go to work-- roll call.
And the sick had to come out from the barrack.
If you have a dead, you had keep bring it for the roll call, you know.
The roll call was the first-- the torture, practically.
Because you had to-- for roll call, you had to stay erect, and you formed, you know, columns, lines.
And you had to be aligned, you know?
If you went out of alignment, you were punished.
The whole row was punished.
And the-- the punishment was, you know this is just, you had this [NON-ENGLISH] and the kapos, you know, step over.
And I hate to tell you, some of the kapos were Jewish, who were long there, probably from Poland.
And they absolutely, they were survivor.
They were like the Nazis.
They were as cruel.
And I'll never forget our kapo.
I hated her.
She was a cruel person.
And I-- I will tell you the thing that she did.
And so-- so we were standing in another punishment.
You know what the punishment was?
The punishment that-- was that the whole row had to kneel in-- in stone or clay.
And you had to keep up your hands like this, as long as they wanted.
And every time, if you were unlucky and the guard noticed you, you were beaten up, because you didn't keep your hand up.
How many times I had the row, this-- this kneeling in the stones?
That was the punishment.
So the morning started already with the-- with the torture.
That was the first torture.
Now, then-- then they numbered us also, when you came back from work.
Work-- what work was-- I told you what work.
To dig the pits and carry the dead, and drag the corpses-- that was our work in Auschwitz when I was there.
They didn't even care anymore to tattoo you, or do anything.
They didn't care, because it was just too much work, you know?
Too much.
You never knew how long you are going to be able to do that work, you know?
They didn't tattoo.
And I say my thanks to God that they didn't brand me at least, you know?
And I don't know, some of my family members who were longer in Auschwitz, they were branded, but I was seven weeks in Auschwitz, you know?
But those seven weeks, I could have died 100 times.
Now, every single week, three times a week, you had the so-called selection.
I say the death sentence, you faced.
Because you had to bash in front of the SS physician.
And if he looked at you, and he found that you are not good anymore to work, he pointed you to the left, which was the gas chamber.
Scurvy broke out, scarlet fever, typhus-- it was-- the camp was infested with disease.
Our-- we got foul water, not to speak what we got.
I would tell you about the food, what we got in Auschwitz.
It was disgusting, what we got in Auschwitz food.
It was the starvation that and you had to do this heavy work.
So-- so that was our roll call.
Now, the food what you got, for instance, in the morning you got black coffee.
And then there was another, I was fortunate not to be assigned to carry this big kettle.
You know, because there were huge kettles.
Because they would assign only a number of people, like two three.
It was a heavy thing.
So that you-- so that you suffer.
I mean, it wasn't that she wanted you to live there-- they wanted you to die.
And they had the-- they would say this.
And they had an enjoyment to see those people.
That they bring these kettle, you know, and this spills.
Sometimes it spills and it burns you.
And then when they go into the camp with the kettle, then many times they're attacked, because everyone wanted that coffee.
And sometimes the whole kettle was-- was poured out, you know.
And people were going on the ground and sucking up the coffee.
I mean it was, you-- you-- this is a different world, you know?
I have seen some people who come who were very nice people when they came out.
And they became-- they lost their humaneness.
But it's no wonder.
I mean, you can't judge this.
Because this is the environment when you want to live.
And the life force, and you desire to survive is writhing over everything, you know?
Now, this was-- was our morning.
What did we get during the day?
Nothing.
We usually get our food in the evening when you came back.
And you had to stay in line again for roll call.
And then we got the six and one half ounce of bread, always stale, hard and black.
And then you got a soup with some rutabaga in it.
I never think that they wash those rutabagas, because you found sand in it, not to speak of the foul water.
You had to close your eyes and close your nostrils in order to be able to somehow swallow that without looking at it and without smelling it.
And then, occasionally they would give you razor-thin slice of some kind of sausage.
We always suspected what it is, you know?
And a spoonful of jelly.
